specification.topic-statement: The input layer renders the agent's text input buffer as terminal rows, preserving newlines and reserving cursor space when the buffer is empty.

scope.in-scope: Visibility determination from an external flag, height calculation by scanning text bytes with column tracking, and rendering that converts newlines to terminal row endings and appends a trailing clear-to-EOL sequence.
scope.boundary: The text content, its byte length, and the visibility flag are managed by the REPL context. This layer stores raw non-owning pointers to live REPL state and reads them at each call.

How the layer's visibility, height, and render functions are invoked by the rendering pipeline is covered by the Layer Rendering specification. This topic covers only the implementations of those three functions for the input layer.
The rendering pipeline calls the visibility predicate once per frame. If it returns false, height and render are not called. If true, height is called to measure the layer, and render is called with a terminal width, a zero-indexed start row within the layer, and a row count.

contract.name: External State (accessed via live raw pointers)

field.name: visible flag
field.note: controls layer visibility; read via pointer at each visibility check

field.name: text pointer
field.note: the input buffer text; read via pointer at each height and render call

field.name: text length
field.note: byte length of the text; read via pointer at each height and render call

behavior.visibility: The layer is visible when the external boolean flag is true. It is invisible when the flag is false.
behavior.height-calculation:
When the text length is zero, the height is 1. A single row is always reserved to provide space for cursor placement, even with no text.
When the text length is non-zero, the height is computed by scanning each byte of the text in order, tracking physical rows and a current column position. Both counters start at 1 and 0 respectively.
For each byte: - If the byte is a newline character (\n): the physical row count is incremented by one, and the column position is reset to zero. - For any other byte: the column position is incremented by one. If the column position equals or exceeds the terminal width, the physical row count is incremented by one and the column position is reset to zero.
The final physical row count is returned as the height.
Each non-newline byte increments the column counter by exactly one, regardless of the byte's role in a multi-byte sequence. Multi-byte UTF-8 characters are counted as multiple columns, not one. ANSI escape sequences are also counted byte-by-byte, inflating the column count for styled text.
behavior.rendering:
The width, start_row, and row_count parameters are accepted by the render function but none of them are used in any part of the output.
The render function's width, start_row, and row_count parameters are all discarded. The full text is always rendered from byte zero, regardless of viewport clipping parameters.
When the text length is zero, the ANSI erase-to-end-of-line sequence (ESC[K) followed by carriage-return and line-feed (\r\n) is appended to the output. This produces a single blank row that preserves the cursor's visual position.
When the text length is non-zero, each byte is processed in order: - A newline character (\n) is replaced by the ANSI erase-to-end-of-line sequence (ESC[K) followed by carriage-return and line-feed (\r\n). - Any other byte is output verbatim as a single byte.
After all bytes have been processed, if the last byte of the text is not a newline, the ANSI erase-to-end-of-line sequence (ESC[K) followed by carriage-return and line-feed (\r\n) is appended to terminate the final partial row.
Lines wider than the terminal width are not truncated, and lines shorter than the terminal width are not padded. The ESC[K sequence erases any stale content from the cursor to the end of the terminal line, which serves as implicit right-side clearing without adding spaces.
